blocks.text: You already know two things: functions can return values, and if/else makes decisions. Now let's combine them.

blocks.alt: A function that contains an if/else and returns a result is one of the most useful patterns in programming: it's exactly how Simon Says will check your answers.
blocks.caption: A function that contains an if/else and returns a result is one of the most useful patterns in programming: it's exactly how Simon Says will check your answers.

blocks.text: A function that contains an if/else and returns a result is one of the most useful patterns in programming: it's exactly how Simon Says will check your answers. In the Simon Says game, the player clicks a sequence of colours.
The game needs to check: did the player get it right? That is a decision — and it happens inside a function called checkResult().
Here is the pattern — a function that makes a decision and returns the answer to the caller:

blocks.label: Simon Says connection
blocks.text: When you finish clicking, the game needs to know: did the player get it right? That question is answered by checkResult(). It compares two things and returns true or false — the caller decides what to do with that answer.

blocks.alt: checkResult() is a function that takes in two values, compares them, and returns true or false. The caller gets that answer back and can act on it.
blocks.caption: checkResult() is a function that takes in two values, compares them, and returns true or false. The caller gets that answer back and can act on it.

blocks.text: Here is an example of `checkResult()`. It takes two values, compares them, and returns true or false. The caller gets that answer back and can act on it.

blocks.label: return stops the function
blocks.text: The moment JavaScript hits a return statement, the function stops. Nothing after it runs. That is why you can have multiple return statements inside an if/else — only one of them will ever fire.

content.heading: In Simon-Says, checkResult() takes the action

blocks.text: The simplified version of `checkResult()` returns true or false.

In Simon-Says, the game goes one step further — instead of returning an answer, `checkResult()` takes the action directly. If the player is correct, it increases the level and starts the next round. If wrong, it ends the game.

blocks.alt: In Simon-Says, checkResult() doesn't return true or false. Instead, it takes the action directly: if the player is correct, it increases the level and starts the next round. If wrong, it ends the game.
blocks.caption: In Simon-Says, checkResult() doesn't return true or false. Instead, it takes the action directly: if the player is correct, it increases the level and starts the next round. If wrong, it ends the game.

blocks.label: Two valid approaches
blocks.text: Both versions use if/else inside a function. The simplified one returns true or false and lets the caller decide what to do. The real one takes the action itself. Neither is wrong — they are just different ways to organise the same decision. You will explore this more in Session 5.

exercises.title: What is this pattern called?
tasks: Which best describes a function that contains an if/else and returns a result?
exercises.hint: Think about what the function does: it receives input, makes a decision, and hands an answer back.

options.label: A loop — it repeats an action
A decision function — it takes input, makes a choice, and returns a result
A variable — it stores a value

exercises.explanation: A function with if/else inside is a decision function. It takes in values, runs a comparison, and returns an answer the caller can use. This is exactly the pattern checkResult() uses in Simon Says.

exercises.title: Which version can the caller use?
tasks: Two versions of the same function. Which one lets the caller store and use the result?
exercises.hint: The caller needs a value it can put into a variable. Which version gives something back?

options.label: Version A — it logs the answer
Version B — it returns the answer
Both — they produce the same result for the caller
Neither — the function needs to use let

exercises.explanation: Version A shows the value on screen but the caller gets undefined. Version B hands the value back so the caller can store it: let result = isMatch('blue', 'blue');

exercises.title: Sort the return values
tasks: Each call to checkResult below produces a result. Drag each one into the correct bucket: true or false.
exercises.hint: === returns true only when both values are exactly equal — same spelling, same case.
exercises.explanation: === is case-sensitive. 'Blue' and 'blue' are different strings, so that call returns false. Even an empty string matches another empty string, so that one returns true.

exercises.title: Complete the if/else inside a function
tasks: Drop the correct tokens to complete this decision function. It should return true when the values match and false when they don't.
exercises.hint: Use === to compare. The if branch handles a match, the else handles a mismatch.
exercises.explanation: Always use === for comparisons — it checks value AND type. The if branch fires on a match and returns true. The else fires on a mismatch and returns false.

exercises.title: Predict the output
tasks: Trace through the code. What does the last console.log print?
exercises.hint: Follow checkResult step by step. What does it return? Where does that value go?
exercises.explanation: 'red' and 'blue' are not equal so the else branch runs and returns false. That false gets stored in result. The log prints 'Match? false'.

exercises.title: Put the decision function in order
tasks: Drag these lines into the correct order to build a working checkResult function and call it.
exercises.hint: Define the function first. Then call it and store the result. Then use the result in an if.
exercises.explanation: The function must be defined before it's called. The return value is stored in result. Then result is used in the if — this is the separation between the decision and the action.

exercises.title: Fill in the full decision function
tasks: Type the missing pieces to complete this checkResult function.
exercises.hint: The comparison operator is three characters. The two return values are the JavaScript booleans for yes and no.
exercises.explanation: === checks strict equality. When a and b match, the if branch fires and returns true. When they don't, the else fires and returns false.

exercises.title: What does this print? (using the result)
tasks: The caller uses checkResult's return value directly in an if. What gets logged?
exercises.hint: What does checkResult('green', 'green') return? Then trace what the if does with that value.
exercises.explanation: checkResult('green', 'green') returns true. The if sees true, so the if branch runs and logs 'Level up!'. You can use a function call directly inside an if — no variable needed.

exercises.title: Two approaches — sort the descriptions
tasks: Drag each description into the correct bucket: 'returns true/false' or 'takes the action directly'.
exercises.hint: One approach lets the caller decide what to do. The other does it itself.

bucketItems.label: The caller decides what to do with the result
Easier to reuse in different situations
The function handles everything in one place
Returns true when the player is correct
Calls level = level + 1 directly inside the function

exercises.explanation: Both are valid patterns. Returning true/false keeps the decision separate from the action — the caller stays in control. Taking the action directly is simpler when there's only ever one thing to do with the result.

exercises.title: Write checkResult from scratch
tasks: Write a function called checkResult that takes playerAnswer and correctAnswer.
If they match, return true. If not, return false.
Call it with ('blue', 'blue') and store the result. Log it with a label.
Call it again with ('red', 'blue') and log that result too.
You should see true then false.
exercises.hint: Use === inside the if. Return true in the if branch, false in the else.

steps.text: Write the checkResult function. Hit Run.
Call it with matching answers and log the result. Hit Run.
Call it with non-matching answers and log that result too. Hit Run.
That's the core of Simon Says — a function that compares two things and hands back a yes or no.

exercises.title: Wire the decision to an action
tasks: checkResult is already written below. Your job is to use it.
Call checkResult('green', 'green') and store the result in a variable called outcome.
Write an if/else that logs 'Level up!' if outcome is true and 'Game over.' if false.
Then change 'green' to 'red' in the call and run it again — you should see 'Game over.'
This is how the caller uses the return value to decide what happens next.
exercises.hint: Store the result first: let outcome = checkResult(...); Then use outcome in your if condition.

steps.text: Call checkResult('green', 'green') and store the result in outcome. Hit Run.
Write if (outcome) { log 'Level up!' } else { log 'Game over.' }. Hit Run.
Now change 'green' to 'red' in the call. Hit Run — you should see 'Game over.'
The decision (checkResult) and the action (if/else) are separate. That's the pattern.

checks.label: Level up! is logged for a match

exercises.title: Build the action-taking version
tasks: This time write a checkResult that takes the action itself — no return value.
If playerClicks === sequence, log 'Correct! Level is now: ' and increase level by 1.
If not, log 'Wrong. Game over.'
Call it twice: once with matching values, once with non-matching.
This mirrors how checkResult() actually works in the Simon Says game.
exercises.hint: No return needed here. Just if/else with console.log and a level variable update inside the if branch.

steps.text: Write the checkResult function that acts directly. Hit Run.
Call checkResult with two matching values. Hit Run.
Call checkResult with two non-matching values. Hit Run.
Same decision — different structure. This version owns the outcome instead of passing it back.

checks.label: Correct! is logged for a match
Game over. is logged for a mismatch

exercises.title: Trace the full game round
tasks: Hit Run and read all the output carefully.
Then change line 10 so playerAnswer is 'blue' instead of 'green'.
Hit Run again and trace what changes.
Finally, add a third round: call playRound('yellow', 'yellow') and run once more.
This is the full pattern: a decision function wired to actions, called multiple times.
exercises.hint: Just run it first and read the output before changing anything. Trace which branch fires each time.

steps.text: Hit Run and read the full output. What happened in each round?
Change line 21 so playerAnswer is 'blue' instead of 'red'. Hit Run.
Add playRound('yellow', 'yellow') on a new line. Hit Run.
Three correct rounds, three level-ups. That's the game loop in action.
